name: Manuel Antonio
summary: Manuel Antonio is one of Costa Rica's most popular and safest beach destinations, offering excellent infrastructure and tourist services. While petty crime and scams targeting tourists have increased in recent years, violent crime remains rare and most safety issues are preventable with basic precautions.
quickVerdict: Manuel Antonio is generally safe for tourists with proper precautions. As Costa Rica's smallest and most popular national park destination, it attracts hundreds of thousands of visitors annually. The main concerns are opportunistic petty theft, tourist-targeted scams, and parking lot cons rather than violent crime. The area has good medical facilities, reliable emergency services, and well-established tourism infrastructure. Solo female travelers, families, and couples can feel secure here by staying alert, using official services, and avoiding displays of wealth.

neighborhoods.name: Manuel Antonio National Park Area
neighborhoods.description: Well-patrolled tourist zone with official parking, guides, and security. Stick to marked trails and use certified tour operators.

neighborhoods.name: Playa Espadilla Norte
neighborhoods.description: Popular beach area with good visibility and regular foot traffic. Watch belongings carefully and avoid leaving items unattended.

neighborhoods.name: Manuel Antonio Town Center
neighborhoods.description: Busy tourist area with restaurants and shops. Generally safe during day but watch for pickpockets in crowded areas.

neighborhoods.name: Quepos
neighborhoods.description: Larger town with more locals than tourists. Exercise normal urban precautions, especially at night and around the market area.

neighborhoods.name: Main Road to Park
neighborhoods.description: Known for parking scams and aggressive vendors. Be cautious of unofficial parking attendants and fake tour guides.

neighborhoods.name: Remote Beaches South
neighborhoods.description: Isolated areas with limited security presence. Avoid walking alone, especially at dawn, dusk, or night. Risk of theft increases significantly.

scams.name: Fake Parking Attendants
scams.description: Unofficial parking operators wearing fake vests direct tourists to expensive lots far from the park entrance, sometimes selling fake park tickets.
scams.howToAvoid: Only use official park parking. Verify parking location and prices beforehand. Never buy tickets from parking attendants - tickets must be purchased online.

scams.name: Unlicensed Tour Guides
scams.description: Uncertified guides approach tourists offering cheap tours but provide poor service, wrong information, or overcharge. Some work with souvenir kickback schemes.
scams.howToAvoid: Only hire certified guides through official tour companies. Check credentials and reviews online. Book through reputable hotels or established tour operators.

scams.name: Overpriced Transport
scams.description: Pirate taxis and unlicensed drivers charge inflated rates or claim broken meters. Some drivers take longer routes to increase fares.
scams.howToAvoid: Use official red taxis with working meters or ride-sharing apps. Agree on fares beforehand for unmetered rides. Ask your hotel for taxi company recommendations.

scams.name: Beach Vendor Pressure
scams.description: Aggressive vendors selling overpriced souvenirs, drinks, or tours may be persistent and sometimes work in groups to distract while accomplices steal.
scams.howToAvoid: Politely but firmly decline unwanted offers. Keep valuables secure and never leave belongings unattended while talking to vendors.

soloFemale.overview: Manuel Antonio is considered one of the safest destinations in Costa Rica for solo female travelers, ranking among the top 25 safest solo female destinations globally according to travel networks. The area has excellent tourism infrastructure, English-speaking staff, and a strong expat community. However, standard precautions for solo travel should always be followed.
tips: Stay in well-reviewed accommodations in central tourist areas rather than remote locations
Use official transportation services and avoid walking alone after dark, especially on isolated beaches
Join group tours or activities to meet other travelers and enhance safety through numbers
Keep drinks in sight at bars and clubs, and inform accommodation staff of your daily plans
Trust your instincts and remove yourself from any uncomfortable situations immediately

nightSafety.overview: Manuel Antonio's nightlife is generally safe in the main tourist areas with visible security presence and good lighting. Most restaurants and bars are concentrated in well-trafficked zones. However, the area quiets down significantly after 9 PM as it's not primarily a party destination. Beach areas become isolated and should be avoided at night.
tips: Stick to main tourist areas with good lighting and avoid walking alone on beaches after dark
Use official taxis or rideshares to return to accommodation rather than walking
Keep emergency contacts and accommodation details easily accessible
Limit alcohol consumption and never leave drinks unattended in bars
Travel in groups when possible and inform someone of your evening plans

transport.metro: No metro system exists in Manuel Antonio. The area relies on buses, taxis, and private transportation.
transport.rideshare: Uber operates in the area and is generally reliable and safe. Drivers are vetted through the app system.
transport.taxis: Official red taxis with yellow triangles are regulated and safe. Ensure meters are working or negotiate fares upfront. Avoid pirate taxis.
transport.tips: Use reputable shuttle services for longer distances. Rental cars are common but parking scams near the national park are frequent. Always verify official parking locations and never leave valuables visible in vehicles.

customs: Greet people warmly with 'Hola' or 'Buenos días' and a smile - Costa Ricans value friendliness
Learn basic Spanish phrases as a sign of respect, though English is widely spoken in tourist areas
Embrace 'Pura Vida' philosophy and don't rush conversations - Costa Ricans prefer relaxed interactions
Dress modestly when away from beaches, especially in town centers and when visiting local establishments
Respect environmental conservation - never litter, don't feed wildlife, and support eco-friendly practices

health.overview: Costa Rica has a well-developed healthcare system with universal coverage. Manuel Antonio area has good access to medical facilities in nearby Quepos and can handle most tourist health issues. Private clinics provide faster service for non-emergency situations.
health.water: Tap water is generally safe to drink in Manuel Antonio and most urban areas, as over 90% of Costa Rica has access to treated water. Those with sensitive stomachs may prefer bottled water.
health.vaccinations: No vaccines are required for entry from the US, but CDC recommends being up-to-date on routine vaccines (MMR, tetanus, hepatitis A). Hepatitis B recommended for close local contact. Consider typhoid vaccine if eating street food.
health.altitude: Manuel Antonio is at sea level. Climate is tropical with high humidity, intense UV radiation due to proximity to equator. Heat exhaustion and sunburn are common tourist health issues. Use strong sunscreen and stay hydrated.

emergency.usEmbassy: For after-hours emergencies: +506-8863-4895 (from Costa Rica) or +1-011-506-8863-4895 (from US). Regular consular services: +506-2519-2000

faq.q: Is Manuel Antonio safe for tourists?
faq.a: Yes, Manuel Antonio is one of Costa Rica's safest tourist destinations with excellent infrastructure and low violent crime rates. Main concerns are petty theft and tourist scams, which are preventable with basic precautions like securing valuables and using official services.

faq.q: Is Manuel Antonio safe at night?
faq.a: The main tourist areas are generally safe at night with good lighting and security presence, but beaches and remote areas should be avoided after dark. Use official transportation and stick to well-lit, populated areas in the evening.

faq.q: Is Manuel Antonio safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Absolutely. Manuel Antonio ranks among the top 25 safest destinations globally for solo female travelers according to travel safety networks. The area has strong tourism infrastructure, English-speaking staff, and active expat communities that enhance safety.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Manuel Antonio?
faq.a: Avoid unofficial parking lots near the national park where scams are common, remote beaches especially after dark, and be cautious on the main road to the park where aggressive vendors operate. Always verify official locations and services.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Manuel Antonio?
faq.a: Public buses are generally safe but keep belongings secure as petty theft can occur. Official red taxis with yellow triangles are reliable, and Uber operates in the area. Avoid unlicensed 'pirate' taxis that may overcharge.
